text: Stressed about trying to get a job | I'm a 15 yr old man and I'm about to have a interview for mcdonalds tomorrow. I heard from a lot of people that its hard and stressful and not worth it. Now I'm wondering if I should even go for the job. Anyone else ever been in the situation and what did you do.

Almost year of experience, having a less than 1% callback rate | anonymized resume - 

https://imgur.com/a/LNQ4R5T

I’ve applied to 10-15 jobs today with this updated resume, so hopefully it’s atleast somewhat good.

I’m in a job that basically ran out of software dev projects for me to do and now it is agonizing. I constantly try to do busy work or just menial tasks and it sucks. last time I asked for more work, I was blown off and kinda just told to suck it up. If I leave early at all, I kinda get questioned about it or told off by my boss, which really sucks and heightens my stress unimaginably because I’m quite literally sitting there doing nothing or trying to do solo coding projects to pass the time.

Now I’m on the hunt for a new job, but even with a little under a year of experience at a consulting company and now here, I’ve sent out 100s of applications, and almost no bites from companies. 

I’m getting extremely depressed and burnt out from applying to job after job with no reply. I’ve been looking for about 2 weeks or so now.

I’ve gotten multiple recruiters, who either ghost me or I get beaten out by another candidate.

My dad has a friend who is a CEO of a tech company who said he can get me on with a friend of his with a great pay/benefits/fully remote, but when I emailed him, he said he would forward me his friend’s info but never did even after I responded, he also said I could get on at his company for something else but once again, he never got back to me.

I’m in a weird situation I guess and maybe just needed to rant a little. Thanks for reading and any help is appreciated

Tips for starting a new job? | When starting a new job, what practices or activities do you do to ensure your success? 

One example is a “new eyes journal” where you write down your observations while you still haven’t gotten used to any of the unfamiliar practices. 

Another would be setting up 1:1s with people in your team to learn about them, their role, and their frustrations. 

Are there other things one should do when starting out?

Edit: it’s a smallish startup, and I’m starting as a tech lead. I have some ideas based on the interviews of what would be helpful, but I want to ensure I build and preserve the capital I’ll need to drive change and be successful.

The worst interview - got hired, now I am scared to show up | I applied for several jobs in the same huge corporation and got called out on it by the hiring manager in my interview.  I took the job but now I feel SO offended for being criticized for APPLYING for other jobs that I am afraid to work there.  I feel like they weren't sure about hiring me and did so with reservations.  I don't want to start this job with the director questioning his decision.  He made me tell him why I wanted to work there.  Maybe I should have made him tell me why he wanted to hire me.  They started the background check but I am thinking of backing out and seeing if I can get an old interview back.  I guess I sound like a flake but frankly I am not getting why someone hired me who was questioning why I was even at the interview so hard.  I don't feel wanted or welcome there, I feel like I was a desperate hire to fill a schedule and I can't stop crying.  All I can think of was the interview I cancelled the day I got hired. :(

EDIT: left some details out - I have no problem with him asking why I want the job.  I just thought it was insensitive to criticize someone for applying for jobs.  I appreciate the advice but I think it's a red flag when employers expect loyalty during a job search.  It's a numbers game.

Please help me understand what the hell is going on? | So an employee refers me to an open role at their workplace. HR never sends me the interview materials they say I’ll need (despite my repeatedly asking), and after the interview, I get rejected from the job (unsurprisingly, since they never sent the materials).

I then reach out to another hiring manager on a different team, and he encourages me to apply for a role on his team when one opens. He says he would love to chat once a role opens. 

Here’s his message:

> Hi John, thanks for reaching out. As I mentioned at the time, I don't have any roles open at the moment, but will likely have one in the coming weeks. Happy to chat more if you are interested once the opening is posted.

Few weeks later, a role opens, and *he sends me* a link to the job posting.

Here’s his message:

> John, that posting is live now. (*Application URL/link*)

I apply, and get promptly rejected by HR without even a phone screen. I then reach out to the hiring manager to tell him I’ve applied and would like to take him up on his offer to chat.  He then says HR will screen all the resumes and reach out to me if they think I’m a good fit.

Here’s his message:

> Thanks for reaching out, John. Our process is that the recruiter will review all resumes and then reach out to let you know if she feels it is a good fit and would like to move forward. Hope you are doing well!

What is going on?

How deeply are you able to understand your work? | So I don't have a ton of work experience, but I am in school right now, and my schedule is very GO GO GO. I feel I'm constantly doing just enough to get assignments and projects done before the deadlines, and if I wanted to understand things better, I simply wouldn't be turning things in by the time they were due. 

I'm hoping when I graduate and my time and energy is less fragmented, that I'll be able to dive into what I'm learning/working on instead of feeling like I'm doing the bare minimum just to scrape by. And if I'm being honest, I really hate working on something and feeling like I don't understand it as well as I'd like to.

Do you feel you're able to do that? How deeply are you able to understand what you're working on? Of course there's always going to be the developers who do the bare minimum, who write sloppy code, etc. So what I'm really asking is: how do I avoid being that sloppy developer after being forced to work like that during school?

Onboarding: asking questions vs needing hand holding? | Newcomers are often encouraged to ask questions while they're onboarding. However, certain questions may seem like you need hand-holding. Generally, you can find out the answer to your question if you just spend time looking things up or spend more time reading through the code. But there's a tradeoff where some things can be answered by an experienced person in a few minutes while it can take hours to look it up.

I'm often having difficulty straddling the line between these two. How do you disambiguate them?   
 I want to be mindful of my colleagues' time but I also want to be efficient.

Is it true that top tech companies companies do not give a darn about what technologies/languages you know but only care about your problem solving abilities? | This is because,they believe that technologies are super easy to learn but  problem solving abilities are very hard to acquire. Is this true?

Do you love what you do for a living? If so, why & how did you get into it? | I just got a new job as a financial representative with a good company, and so far it’s been good. Overall I like it, but I’m not yet sure that I really LOVE the work. Do you love your job?

“Unlimited PTO” - what does a healthy amount of PTO look like? | Starting a new job next week that offers “unlimited PTO”, and from all accounts I’ve heard so far, the company genuinely does encourage you to take time off without making you feel guilty for it (what I’ve experienced in the past with “unlim PTO”). 

But what is a “healthy” amount of PTO without overdoing it? Is it taking 2-3 days off per month? 1-week every couple months? I’m very much at a loss with this concept


Edit: I can’t believe how much this blew up! Hope this helps everybody else in a similar predicament! 

Cheers

Professional job interview today. I have been homeless and only have jeans to wear. | UPDATE:. Hey guys! I want to thank you for all the suggestions and encouragement and support I have received today! It was a huge ego boost. The interview really wasn't much of an interview. The Execution Director handed me the background paperwork, which I filled out. He came back out to talk to me and said they didn't hire RNs, but only LPNs???? He knew I was an RN. I explained I had applied specifically because I have always wanted to work with assisted living residents. You aren't as busy and you get a lot more time with them. He asked if I would take LPN pay. I said yes. The Director of Nursing was coming by as I was leaving and he introduced me. I am hired pending my background. I do have an interview tomorrow at another facility right down the road. Luckily I will have actual dress clothes thanks to you all!! I really am floored by the cheerleading I've received. It really has helped my self-esteem after the stuff I've been trying to get corrected! My background is clean so I should be good! 


This is pretty short. I have nice clothes, but only have a small amount on me. Luckily, my Dad let me put my stuff in his storage back in October. He doesn't like me otherwise and I have no way to get to my clothes today. 

I am homeless and do have nice tops, boots, my makeup, but only jeans. Luckily I have secured myself a way to shower and get ready today. 

This is a brand new facility in our area and very uppity. I work in healthcare so I am pretty worried. My resume' was already complete garbage. My highlights have also grown out and I've gained 11 lbs (I'm really petite) so my weight is up. I feel so gross. 

I gained weight due to poor diet..eating mainly canned food since November. Sometimes I can purchase other things, but not always. 

I'm so worried. I really need to get back to work and I finally can. My last attempt in January suffered due to lack of transportation. (Long, frustrating story). 

I just feel like such a loser and so disgusting. I don't know why I didn't preplan over the weekend or yesterday!! I've had so much happen in 4 days, it never came to mind. 

Has this ever happened to anyone else?

Edit: Thanks for all the encouraging comments! I'm feeling a lot more confident. I will update this evening once it's over!

[OFFICIAL] Salary Sharing thread for EXPERIENCED DEVS :: June, 2022 | **MODNOTE:** Some people like these threads, some people hate them. If you hate them, that's fine, but please don't get in the way of the people who find them useful. Thanks!

This thread is for sharing recent new grad offers you've gotten or current         The young'ins had their chance, now it's time for us geezers to shine! This thread is for sharing recent offers/current salaries for professionals with **2 or more years of experience**.

Please only post an offer if you're including hard numbers, but feel free to use a throwaway account if you're concerned about anonymity. You can also genericize some of your answers (e.g. "Biotech company" or "Hideously Overvalued Unicorn"), or add fields if you feel something is particularly relevant.

* Education:
* Prior Experience:
    * $Internship
    * $RealJob
* Company/Industry:
* Title:
* Tenure length:
* Location: 
* Salary: 
* Relocation/Signing Bonus:
* Stock and/or recurring bonuses:
* Total comp:
        
Note that you only really need to include the relocation/signing bonus into the total comp if it was a recent thing. Also, while the primary purpose of these threads is obviously to share compensation info, discussion is also encouraged.

The format here is slightly unusual, so **please make sure to post under the appropriate top-level thread**, which are: US [High/Medium/Low] CoL, Western Europe, Eastern Europe, Latin America, Aus/NZ, Canada, Asia, or Other.

**If you don't work in the US, you can ignore the rest of this post.** To determine cost of living buckets, I used this site: http://www.bestplaces.net/

If the principal city of your metro is not in the reference list below, go to bestplaces, type in the name of the principal city (or city where you work in if there's no such thing), and then click "Cost of Living" in the left sidebar. The buckets are based on the Overall number: [Low: < 100], [Medium: >= 100, < 150], [High: >= 150]. (last updated Dec. 2019)
        
High CoL: NYC, LA, DC, SF Bay Area, Seattle, Boston, San Diego
        
Medium CoL: Orlando, Tampa, Philadelphia, Dallas, Phoenix, Chicago, Miami, Atlanta, Riverside, Minneapolis, Denver, Portland, Sacramento, Las Vegas, Austin, Raleigh

Low CoL: Houston, Detroit, St. Louis, Baltimore, Charlotte, San Antonio, Pittsburgh, Cincinnati, Kansas City

Juniors/New Grads: Be Annoying!

Why do so many newer devs come in having never used a shell or command line interface? | I'm 12 years into my career and a heavy user of bash/shell or anything from the command line. For just about everything other than debugging tools I find it easier and faster to do it from the command line. That said, it's my preference and I know everyone has their own preference for tools!  


However it seems to me like most new developers out of college have never touched a shell and it is somewhat handicapping them. For example, I have met new developers who don't really understand what ssh, ftp or scp, thread monitoring cmds, etc. is outside of using whatever GUI tool they use to do it. I find every single time I can grep for something and get a better picture of the code structure than they can by using the IDE  inspection tools. I think just using GUIs coddles you into not knowing how to get in the dirt and find or do something yourself. At the same time, GUIs and IDEs certainly have their place and many times you can no longer get by or be as effective by just using vim or other editor. Modern IDEs with code completion and other features can you make a faster developer than just using an editor alone, certainly.  


I give an example now where I have just switched jobs and am onboarding with two other developers, both who are as senior as I am but have no shell experience. They have been waiting for people to show them how to use the tools here and walk them through everything for two weeks while I am already working in the code and getting tasks done. I already have a very good understanding of the code base here even though it is entirely new to me. I'm not smart, I just credit it to the years of practice navigating large code bases with a shell and reading code the old fashioned way.  


Just a thought, and I'm sorry if this sounds like boomer snobbery, I don't mean it to be. I've mentored several brand new developers and really pushed them to work in a terminal and every one of them has thanked me for it and even reached out for advice after I left.   


I guess what I want to say to new developers is, since so many of you are new, if you want to be more effective at your job, don't be afraid to use a terminal!

Edit: I want to mention there are some extremely sharp and effective developers who use almost entirely GUIs for everything. But then you have the other kind, like one I am working with now, who is supposed to be pair programming with me. They have been stuck for 4 days doing nothing because their IDE of choice that nobody else here uses keeps doing something weird to their project and they are unable to fix it, adapt or move forward. This is the kind of developer who really needs to spend some time learning about different tools, because they end up wasting other devs’ time when the tools they know well don’t work.

Does my request for bereavement leave seem unreasonable/refusal worthy? | I (M21) work a part time job at a grocery store. My best friend pass away today due to cancer. I have known he has been in declining health for a few weeks, and that at any moment he could die. As such, I called out of work the previous weekend (Saturday/Sunday) to go visit him and see at least once before he dies. My GM gave me shit for it but I don’t really mind that too much, my main concern would be now that my friend has actually died, will calling out affect whether or not I qualify for bereavement leave? I would be asking to have Saturday and Sunday off again (I already have Friday off). I also would like to know if bereavement leave is typically denied for people that aren’t immediate family/relatives. This death hurts a lot and has been affecting my work, and pretty much every area of my life. I have pretty much completely lost my drive to work for the past 2 weeks, and really need time to process this.

Former Manager Keeps Calling & Texting Me with Questions After Quitting | Need some advice on this situation. My former manager has been calling and texting me multiple times a week with questions relating to projects and tasks for my job.

Here are some details:
- I quit 2 months ago and have been receiving multiple texts and calls a week from my manager, occasionally other coworkers relating to my job and other ongoing projects. 
- I left thorough documentation behind when I left, definitely above and beyond what previous employees left behind
- I gave 3 weeks notice and informed my manager of my plans to leave prior to giving notice
- I know my manager outside of work, this is what makes this more difficult than simply not responding at all. I liked him a lot as a manager and person, that is not why I left. 
- Small company, less than 10 people.
- I did not enjoy working at this company, nor did I think the CEO was doing a good job managing. Incredibly high turnover rate.
- I trained multiple consultants who were taking over for me as well as a new hire EA in my final week.
- I’m a few years out of college and this was my first time quitting a job. 
- i have already begun my next endeavor, and even offered my manager a couple hours of consulting a week (on weekends) if he needed additional help. I did not really want to do it, but offered to be friendly. While he initially said he was interested, he has since bypassed that and just called and texted with questions.
- the questions are not particularly difficult to answer, but still take up time nonetheless 

Is this standard practice when leaving a job, and I should just answer questions and respond to the best of my ability?

Or should I be handling this another way?

Any help would be greatly appreciated.
Anyone here working in Goldman Sachs?

With the recent layoffs, it's become increasingly obvious that what team you're on is really important to your job security | For the most part, all of the recent layoffs have focused more on shrinking sectors that are less profitable, rather than employee performance. 10k in layoffs didn't mean "bottom 10k engineers get axed" it was "ok Alexa is losing money, let's layoff X employees from there, Y from devices, etc..." And it didn't matter how performant those engineers were on a macro level.


So if the recession is over when you get hired at a company, and you notice your org is not very profitable, it might be in your best interest to start looking at internal transfers to more needed services sooner rather than later. Might help you dodge a layoff in the future

My old boss keeps texting me asking me questions after I’ve left job | I left my job 3 weeks ago on fine terms. They were surprised, but everything was professional. 

My direct boss really was bummed to see my go (but was really a large reason I left). Again, we left on good terms. He wished me well and was super appreciative of the work I’ve done. 

He’s texted me about 5 times now to ask me for thing that I put in my document laying out everything before I left, and as to not burn a bridge, I responded and helped. I did reach out to him once to get myself removed from one of their profiles and went back and forth a ton, until he just stopped replying, so I just transferred the account ownership to someone on the account because he didn’t help me. 

The things he’s asking me aren’t complicated to the point where I feel it’s appropriate to say I need to bill you for my time, but I’m getting frustrated. He just texted me three more times asking me about something I put in my document, saying that so and so said I never sent it and that’s not like me, and then asking me a question he needs to be asking IT, even though I know the answer. 

I don’t feel like it’s my responsibility to answer or help or explain myself, but like I said, it’s not that’s much work to answer the question where I should tell him I would have to charge them as a consultant. Also, it low key pisses me off that he’s insinuating that if I didn’t “do” the task (which I didn’t and clearly outlined in my document and explained to him why) that he’s casting judgement on my character. 

I don’t want to burn a bridge, but I also don’t want to keep answering because I know it won’t stop if I do. I feel like if I say nicely I can’t help you, he’ll take it very offensively and it’ll seem like I’m unreasonable to not want to answer questions/explain things. 

Any advice on how to nicely ask him to stop reaching out?

I'm torn between STEM (CS & Stats) and the Arts as a sophomore in college--what insights do you have? | I'm a sophomore in college studying statistics (emphasis on data science) right now. I don't think I really enjoy it though. I really enjoyed ap stats in HS and it made a ton of sense to me but ever since coming to college, I feel like I'm not getting it (I've done the basic stats, theory of interest, and statistical modeling 1). I don't know if I'm struggling with my professors or if maybe this just isn't for me. I have a ton of passions but they don't all connect in a way that leads me down one specific career path. In high school, I really wanted to be an architect (the college I'm at doesn't have a program, otherwise I would probably be doing this), then an elementary school teacher, then an art teacher, then an actuary (not for me haha), then sports stats, and now I'm finding that I really enjoy coding (I'm in an entry-level class that I find very easy, so maybe that's why I enjoy it?) At the moment, I'm thinking about going into CS or ArtEd or just staying where I'm at because I'm about 40 credits deep into my major and I'm afraid that changing to the arts will extend my college career drastically--but that would be worth it if I end up hating what I'm currently doing. 

I love baseball (watching, not playing haha), art (painting, drawing, anything creative), design (interior or just general), and coding (I have a little bit of experience in Python). What careers paths/majors might you suggest?

What are some ways to make a new boss extremely glad to have hired me? Both big and little things. | Edit: I just want to illustrate that I’m someone that’s responsible and is able to get the job done without being fake or a kiss-ass. I have beef with my current boss and it sucks. 

Edit 2: (I should’ve added this in the beginning for some context. It’s a $120k/year sales job where after a few year would likely get to $200k/year and it generally 40 hour work weeks with some overtime. So working over time we get paid extra AND it gives more time to make sales. Honestly, I’m just a perfectionist and like to make lists. This probably isn’t necessary but suggestions here help me (and probably others reading it) visualize how I want to be in my next role. )


I’ll try to add to this list as I see comments.

While a lot of things are common sense I think it’s helpful to have lists that serve as a reminder. It’s easy to get comfortable and complacent.

I’ll start (and ad more as they come in):
- Always early
- Always willing to work a bit later and help out
- Showing deference: eg. prioritize responding to boss
- Don’t ask questions until you’ve already researched available sources
- Just a general attitude of being grateful to be there.
- Taking pride in being a good employee
- Never complain. (While not being a pushover)


Edit: Added from comments below
- Don’t gossip
- Be meticulous with adding activity to CRM (sales)
- Write things down so you never ask what’s already been said
- Generally positive, caring, empathetic
- Learn other positions
- Say yes when other say no. (Go getter).
- Accept positive constructive criticism. 
- Communicate wins, progress, and road blocks. 



Those are some bigger things but I’ll add smaller things as well. 

The job I’m about to get is a high paying sales job and so more of a solo job. Not much teamwork. But still, I’ve been on bad terms with my recent boss, despite being successful, and it definitely sucks. Unnecessary stress and bad vibes for sure. So I want to make sure I do it right at the next place.

I am embarrassed to check in a bugfix | I have been working on this really simple bug where basically the order of two variables are switched in an email notification. The issue is, the notification process is a 10000 line pile of spaghetti dog shit. I spent a week just debugging the pile of dog shit just to understand what was going on and possible edge cases. The only way(I could think of) to fix this ‘simple’ bug without introducing many more bugs was to refactor the entire thing, my business logic isn’t at a point where I felt comfortable rewriting the whole thing or else that would have been best. 

I started refactoring it, and about half way through I realized I introduced more bugs and understood why the original code looked the way it did(not that it’s justified, it should have been refactored years ago instead of just throwing duct tape on it). I’ve asked my team and this person for help and everyone either knows nothing about it or won’t touch it with a 10ft pole.

I fixed the original bug and the ones I introduced but now my code is half the original dog shit and half clean code and I am not confident in the rigidity of it but have spent more time than I am willing to testing it anymore. I am embarrassed to check it in/get it reviewed with my name on it but i’m also embarrassed to work with my teammate who wrote that code.  

If this isn’t an excuse for me to rant I guess my question would be how do I get over the embarrassment, how do I prevent this in the future and how do you approached fixing spaghetti code?

What should I prioritize while job hunting? | I graduated with a BS in Computer Science in March after struggling to finish my degree during quarantine. I’ve been searching for jobs even before I graduated, but it hasn’t worked out and it’s hard to get interviews. 

I’ve sent out about 100 cold applications through job portals and 10 applications with referrals. I have referrals from people that graduated a year or two earlier than me from their companies. I even got a referral to LM and got a rejection through their portal two days later. I’m a Latina that’s a citizen of the US (which where I’m applying for jobs), though I don’t know if that affects anything. 

I think my largest resume flaw is my lack of professional internships. Not to get too personal, but I was in an abusive situation all throughout college. It left me with mental health issues that made it hard to function and I struggled to graduate. I’m away from that situation now, but I’m struggling to get back on my feet professionally.

So far, I’ve had two interviews.  One with a startup I talked to at a career fair. I didn’t pass their second interview where they gave me three hours to make a game that met their specifications. The other was with google after applying to an early career position that didn’t require a college degree. My interviewer says I can re-interview in December, but I’m nervous about getting closer to the 1 year mark of unemployment.

Am I approaching job hunting the wrong way? I struggle with prioritization, so I’m not sure wether I should focus most of my energy on personal projects or changing my application strategy.

Pay differences in Chicago vs Bay Area/Seattle | I am looking for a place to settle down for at least the next few years, and I'm looking at either Chicago or one of the two west coast tech hubs.

I like Chicago for many reasons, including its **drastically** lower housing costs. I also don't pay property taxes there, being a veteran.

That being said, [levels.fyi](https://levels.fyi) shows median salaries being quite lower in Chicago. Chicago shows $138k vs 210k and 203k for SF and Seattle.

How well do these numbers hold up in reality? For someone working in a FAANG+ company, would the difference be much smaller? I know that the west coast has a ton of high-paying startups, so that might edge up their numbers a bit.

How to earn money as a brazillian teenager that knows english, programming and Linux? | Hello, I'm a 16 year old brazillian that is looking into ways of earning money by only knowing english, programming and Linux.

My journey into learning programming started in 2017, when I was 11, with making games. Nowdays, I know/have experience with C/C++, C#, Java, JavaScript, Python, Rust and GDScript (Godot Engine's language). I also know how Linux works for desktop usage and etc. Mostly Arch Linux and these do-it-yourself distros. I learned these in a natural way, mostly on the internet and/or YouTube. I haven't learned with paid courses. All of these things are just my hobby and interest, so why not using my knowledge to some profit?

I know game dev with Godot Engine, and I also tried making an entire game engine from scratch (not an easy task but it's fun to try).

I landed my dream job. But that’s because my idea of a dream job changed. | I have always worked for startups or high- growth, fast-paced companies. Pre-pandemic, I thought I had landed my dream job: I landed a job where they offered so many office outings and perks like a masseuse coming in once a quarter and Taco Tuesdays and free lunch. I also had jobs I thought were dream jobs because they had a cool downtown location or because the team was close-knit and went to happy hours a lot. Pay was decent, but not where I wanted it to be, and I didn’t really have opportunities to grow (though they promised I would).

Today I got a job offer and realized, for the very first time in my career, I truly landed my dream job. It’s completely remote (which is something I’ve come to realize I need for my mental and physical health), the pay is 30% more than my current job (which will help while I’m starting to save for my future), and I’ll be transitioning to my actual dream career within 3 months of being there (software engineer).

I don’t know how I could have ever believed beer on tap and ping pong tables were what made a company desirable to work for. Flexible work environments, higher pay, etc…this is what I’m looking for.
